reasoning: Exact blocking on artist+name was far too restrictive (PC=0.364). Semantic blocking previously on name alone achieved decent PC (0.873) within the candidate budget (226,076 < 350,000). To improve PC while keeping candidates predictable, use semantic_similarity with a richer text signal by embedding both artist and name together; keep top_k fixed at 10 per constraint so candidates stay ~left_size*10 (≈226k).

Previous semantic_similarity on (artist,name,release-date) nearly met PC (0.949) with 225,778 candidates under the 350k cap. To significantly change parameters while staying within the strict top_k=10 rule, drop release-date (has 7–10% nulls and can harm recall if formats differ/missing) and embed only artist+name to increase robustness to date issues and boost PC while keeping candidates predictable at left_size×top_k (~226,270).

With ~47M potential pairs and a 350k candidate cap, exact match is likely too brittle due to formatting differences (e.g., 'John B - Fermats Theorem / Sight Beyond' vs 'Fermats Theorem / Sight Beyond'), while token/ngram blocking on name would risk large candidate sets and still miss reworded variants. Embedding-based semantic blocking on combined 'name' and 'artist' is robust to punctuation/order differences and keeps candidates predictable at left_size×top_k ≈ 4,763×10 = 47,630, well under the limit.
